fast.summary: This paper proposes Cumulative Cross-Entropy (CCE) loss for sequential recommendation, which computes cross-entropy loss across all positions in a training sequence rather than just the last timestamp. CCE eliminates negative sampling entirely while achieving large improvements over BPR, BCE, and standard CE loss on multiple benchmarks.
fast.key_idea: By accumulating cross-entropy loss over every step in the training sequence instead of only the final position, CCE maximally utilizes sequence information and avoids the gradient and sampling pathologies of BPR and BCE losses.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners can drop-in replace their loss function in existing sequential recommendation models (GRU4Rec, SASRec, S3-Rec) to get 45–84% NDCG improvements with no negative sampling overhead and faster convergence, making training both simpler and more effective.
possible_use_cases: Improving product recommendation quality in e-commerce by swapping CCE into existing sequential models without architectural changes
Accelerating model training cycles for user behavior modeling in streaming or real-time recommendation pipelines
Enhancing next-item prediction in content platforms (video, music, news) where long user interaction histories are available

deep.method_breakdown: CCE computes cross-entropy loss at every position in the training sequence rather than only at the final timestamp, treating each intermediate item as a prediction target against the full item vocabulary without negative sampling. For a sequence of length T, the loss sums CE terms across all T positions, where each position predicts the next item using a softmax over all items. This forces the model to learn from every transition in the sequence, maximizing information utilization from available training data.
deep.key_innovation: Prior work either uses BPR/BCE with negative sampling (introducing bias and sensitivity to sample count) or CE applied only to the last sequence position (wasting intermediate supervision signal). CCE's novelty is the combination of full-softmax cross-entropy (eliminating sampling bias) applied cumulatively across all sequence positions (eliminating the single-step bottleneck), yielding a loss that is both unbiased and maximally data-efficient without architectural changes.
deep.technical_insight: The critical design choice is computing softmax over the entire item vocabulary at each sequence step rather than sampling negatives. Full-softmax CE provides a proper probability distribution and avoids the gradient vanishing that plagues BPR under many negatives, while the cumulative summation across positions means the gradient signal is proportional to sequence length — longer sequences provide richer training signal, naturally regularizing against sparse interaction patterns.
deep.limitations: Full-softmax over the entire item catalog scales as O(|V|) per position per sequence step, making it computationally prohibitive for very large item catalogs (millions of items) common in industrial settings. The method is validated only on relatively small benchmark datasets; the reported wall-clock improvements may not hold at scale. Additionally, treating every position equally may over-weight early, less informative transitions in long sequences, and the approach assumes the next-item prediction objective is appropriate for all sequence types.
interview_questions: Why does BPR loss suffer vanishing gradients with many negative samples, and how does CCE avoid this?
What is the computational complexity of CCE compared to CE and BCE, and when does it become a bottleneck?
Why is applying CE only at the last sequence position suboptimal, and what information does CCE recover?
How would you extend CCE to handle very large item catalogs (e.g., 10M+ items) in production?
Could applying equal loss weight to all sequence positions be harmful, and how might you address it?
interview_answers: BPR optimizes pairwise rankings via sigmoid(pos_score - neg_score). With many negatives, most pairs have small score differences after training converges, pushing the sigmoid into saturation and producing near-zero gradients. CCE uses full-softmax CE which maintains non-zero gradients as long as the target item is not assigned probability 1, and the gradient magnitude scales with prediction error across all items simultaneously.

CCE is O(T * |V|) per training sequence versus O(|V|) for standard CE and O(T * k) for BCE with k negatives. For small catalogs (tens of thousands of items) and typical sequence lengths (< 200), this is tractable. It becomes a bottleneck when |V| exceeds ~500K, where approximate softmax methods (sampled softmax, hierarchical softmax, or product quantization) would be needed.

Standard CE on only the last position uses one supervision signal per sequence regardless of length, so a sequence of 50 items provides the same gradient update count as a sequence of 2 items. CCE recovers T-1 additional prediction targets from the same sequence, proportionally increasing the effective training set size and forcing the encoder to produce meaningful intermediate representations at every step, not just the final hidden state.

The main approaches are: (1) sampled softmax — approximate the full softmax using a subset of negatives drawn from a noise distribution, with importance weighting to reduce bias; (2) adaptive softmax — cluster items by frequency into hierarchical buckets; (3) in-batch negatives — treat other items in the same batch as negatives, which scales independently of |V|. A hybrid of in-batch negatives with hard negative mining would preserve most of CCE's benefits at industrial scale.

Yes — early positions in a sequence often reflect older, less relevant preferences, and weighting them equally may dilute the signal from recent transitions. A position-decay weighting (e.g., linear or exponential discount toward earlier positions) could address this, similar to discounting in reinforcement learning. Alternatively, one could apply CCE only to the latter half of each sequence or use attention-based weighting that learns which positions carry more predictive value.
